channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Region
channel.description: Uplifting stories from every corner of the world, organised by region.

item.title: Photographers Document Record 304-Whale Humpback Supergroup Off South Africa
item.description: Wildlife photographers documented 304 individual humpback whales in a single feeding supergroup off South Africa — a world record for baleen whales observed together. The encounter reflects the remarkable population recovery of humpbacks from near-extinction during the whaling era, with scientists now estimating 125,000 worldwide.

item.title: History Made: All Six 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize Winners Are Women
item.description: For the first time in the Goldman Prize's 37-year history, all six recipients are women — including Nigeria's Iroro Tanshi, who protected an endangered bat's habitat through community action, and South Korea's Borim Kim, who won Asia's first youth-led climate lawsuit. The all-female class spans six continents and six landmark victories.

item.title: 900 Przewalski's Wild Horses Roam Free in China After 40 Years of Rewilding
item.description: China's conservation programme has restored 900 Przewalski's horses — the last truly wild equine species — to steppes across Xinjiang, Inner Mongolia, and Gansu. From extinction in the wild in 1985, this 40-year effort now sustains one-third of the global population, inspiring the mascot for China's 2026 Year of the Horse celebrations.

item.title: Asian Cities Lead Global Wave Slashing Air Pollution by Up to 40%
item.description: Research finds 19 major cities worldwide have cut air pollution 20–40% over 15 years, with nearly half in Asia and nine in China. Sustained investment in clean transport, industrial regulation, and green urban planning is delivering measurable public health gains across the continent's most densely populated regions.

item.title: Renewables Met Every Gigawatt of New Global Electricity Demand in 2025
item.description: In a landmark milestone, renewable sources covered all new global electricity demand in 2025 for the first time, pushing fossil fuels into structural decline. Solar led the surge, with China and India recording their steepest-ever drops in fossil fuel generation — a turning point analysts say cannot be reversed.

item.title: One-Time Gene Therapy Saves Six-Year-Old Saffie from Inherited Blindness
item.description: Six-year-old Saffie Sandford received the Luxturna gene therapy at Great Ormond Street Hospital in London, reversing a rare inherited condition that causes progressive blindness. The single treatment corrects a faulty retinal gene and has already transformed Saffie's vision — offering hope to children worldwide with the same diagnosis.

item.title: New Electric Vehicles Are Now Cheaper Than Petrol Cars in the UK
item.description: The average price of a new EV in the UK has fallen below that of a new petrol car — £42,620 vs £43,405 — driven by government grants and manufacturer discounts. The price crossover marks a tipping point for mainstream adoption and signals that the economics of clean transport have fundamentally shifted.

item.title: Metal Detectorists Unearth Norway's Largest-Ever Viking Age Coin Hoard
item.description: Hobbyist metal detector enthusiasts in Norway have discovered the country's largest collection of Viking-age coins, a find historians are calling historic. The trove sheds new light on Norse trade networks and medieval Scandinavian wealth, with the detectorists praised for responsibly reporting the discovery to archaeological authorities.

item.title: US Renewables Beat Natural Gas for the First Time in History
item.description: In March 2026, US wind, solar, hydropower, and biomass combined generated 35% of the country's electricity — surpassing natural gas at 34% for the first time ever. By end of 2026, renewable installed capacity is projected to exceed gas capacity entirely, driven by record solar and battery additions.

item.title: Cooper Flagg, 19, Becomes Youngest Player in NBA History to Score 50 Points
item.description: Dallas Mavericks rookie Cooper Flagg scored 51 points on April 3 to become the first teenager in NBA history to reach 50 in a single game. The 19-year-old's dazzling fourth-quarter display — 24 points after his coach was ejected — announced the arrival of a once-in-a-generation talent.

item.title: Five Students Jump Into Action and Save Their Unconscious School Bus Driver
item.description: When their school bus driver suddenly lost consciousness at the wheel, five students worked together to steer the bus safely to a stop and call for help, preventing a catastrophic accident. Their calm, decisive teamwork is being celebrated as an extraordinary example of courage and community.

item.title: COP30 in Belém Secures Historic $1.3 Trillion Annual Climate Finance Commitment
item.description: The COP30 climate summit, hosted by Brazil in Belém, concluded with nations adopting the Belém Political Package — committing to mobilise at least $1.3 trillion per year by 2035. The deal tripled adaptation finance pledges and operationalised the loss and damage fund, marking the most significant climate finance breakthrough since Paris.

item.title: Tropical Forests Forever Fund Reaches $5.5 Billion With 53 Nations Committed
item.description: Built on COP30's momentum, the Tropical Forests Forever Facility has raised $5.5 billion from 53 countries, with at least 20% of resources directed to Indigenous Peoples and local communities. The fund places tropical forest protection — including the Amazon — on the most financially secure footing in history.

item.title: Colombian Activist Wins 'Green Nobel' for Permanently Halting Commercial Fracking
item.description: Yuvelis Morales Blanco won the 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize after leading the campaign that prevented commercial fracking from taking hold in Colombia. Her coalition — built through community organising and legal challenges against major oil companies — delivered a permanent victory for Colombian land and water.

item.title: Australia's Renewables Hit Record 47% of Grid Power in Q1 2026
item.description: Australia's national electricity grid set a first-quarter record in 2026, with renewables supplying 47% of generation while coal fell to an all-time Q1 low. Grid-scale solar jumped 13% year-on-year, and batteries became the most frequent price-setting technology — a decisive acceleration in the clean energy transition.

item.title: Australia's Electric Vehicle Fleet Surpasses Half a Million With Record March Sales
item.description: Australia passed 500,000 registered electric vehicles in March 2026, with that month setting a new national sales record. Batteries now shape electricity prices in 32% of dispatch intervals — the most frequent price-setting technology on the grid — showing the EV transition is simultaneously reshaping roads and energy markets.

item.title: Oceania's Solar, Hydrogen, and Battery Revolution Is Becoming a Global Model
item.description: A new analysis highlights Oceania's extraordinary alignment of natural advantages — abundant solar, vast wind corridors, and proximity to energy-hungry Asian economies — propelling the region toward clean energy leadership. Australia and New Zealand are emerging as global models for integrated solar-battery-hydrogen grid transformation.

item.title: Antarctic Ice Unlocks a 3-Million-Year Climate Mystery That Challenges Scientific Models
item.description: Scientists studying ancient Antarctic ice cores found that while the planet cooled dramatically over 3 million years, CO₂ and methane levels changed surprisingly little — suggesting ice sheets, ocean circulation, and Earth's reflectivity drove far more of long-term climate change than previously understood. The findings will reshape climate models.

item.title: COOKIES Expedition Maps 400 Species in East Antarctica's Little-Known Waters
item.description: The COOKIES research voyage — the most comprehensive study of the marine ecosystem around East Antarctica's Cook Ice Shelf — documented around 400 organisms and captured over 10,000 deep-towed camera images. The survey delivers a vital biodiversity baseline for one of Earth's least-studied ocean regions.

item.title: Algeria Becomes 29th Nation to Eradicate Trachoma
item.description: The World Health Organization officially validated Algeria's elimination of trachoma — the world's leading infectious cause of blindness — making it the 10th African nation and 29th globally to achieve this milestone. Decades of surgery, antibiotics, and sanitation improvements freed millions from this entirely preventable disease.

item.title: Fela Kuti Is Africa's First Artist in the Rock Hall of Fame
item.description: Nigerian Afrobeat pioneer Fela Kuti was named to the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame Class of 2026, making him the first African artist ever to receive this honour. His politically charged, joyful music inspired generations — a long-overdue recognition crossing continents and decades.

item.title: African Union Unites 55 Nations to Transform Education by 2035
item.description: Education ministers, UN agencies, and development partners gathered in Nairobi for the African Union's Continental CESA Forum, aligning 55 nations behind a shared blueprint to transform teaching, expand digital access, and build skills for economic growth. It marks the continent's most unified push for educational equity in a generation.

item.title: India's Fast Breeder Reactor Achieves Historic First Criticality
item.description: India's 500 MW Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam reached its first self-sustaining nuclear reaction — a landmark in the country's three-stage programme toward thorium-based energy independence. The reactor is designed to breed more fuel than it consumes, potentially powering India for centuries.

item.title: Wild Pangolins Bounce Back in Southern China After Six Years of Protection
item.description: Six years after China elevated the Chinese pangolin to its highest conservation tier, a new survey counts 1,778 individuals in Guangdong Province — a marked recovery for a species that had lost over 80% of its population. A dedicated national breeding centre has now been established to secure the species' future.

item.title: South Korea Posts Fastest Economic Growth in Five Years
item.description: South Korea's GDP expanded 1.7% in Q1 2026 — nearly double forecasts — driven by a 5.1% surge in semiconductor exports and strong business investment. Analysts raised the full-year 2026 growth outlook to 2.8%, signalling renewed economic confidence across the region.

item.title: Europe Shatters Its Own Clean Energy Record in Q1 2026
item.description: Europe generated 384.9 TWh of renewable electricity in Q1 2026 — an all-time record — with wind power up 22% and solar up 15% year-on-year. Portugal alone ran on renewables for 571 hours of the quarter, covering 78.5% of its mainland electricity needs.

item.title: Orkney's Stoat Eradication Brings Native Wildlife Roaring Back
item.description: Scotland's Orkney Native Wildlife Project has removed over 8,500 invasive stoats since 2019, and the results are extraordinary: Orkney vole activity is at its highest ever recorded spring level, hen harrier breeding is at a decade high, and short-eared owls have returned in force. Full eradication is expected by end of 2027.

item.title: World's First Offshore Ocean Thermal Power Platform Switched On
item.description: UK company Global OTEC successfully installed the world's first purpose-built offshore platform generating electricity from ocean temperature differences, tested at Spain's Canary Islands. The breakthrough cuts the required pipe length by 80%, with potential to replace over 25 GW of fossil fuel power on tropical islands worldwide.

item.title: US Fast-Tracks Psychedelic Therapies to Help Veterans Heal
item.description: A new executive order directed the FDA to prioritise review of three psychedelic drugs showing breakthrough potential for PTSD and depression, backed by million in research funding. Called the most significant federal action on psychedelics in US history, it opens a promising new frontier for mental health care.

item.title: Canadian Scientist Patents Clean Hydrogen Produced Directly From Seawater
item.description: University of Alberta professor Steve Bergens patented a process that produces green hydrogen directly from seawater — eliminating the costly desalination step that has held back the technology. Licensed by Canadian company Cipher Neutron, the breakthrough could dramatically lower the cost of clean fuel at scale.

item.title: Boston Marathon Runners Stop to Carry Collapsed Competitor to Finish
item.description: When Northwestern student Ajay Haridasse's legs gave out at mile 26, British runner Aaron Beggs and Brazil's Robson De Oliveira locked arms with him and carried him across the finish line, sacrificing their own times. The moment went globally viral as a celebration of sport at its most human.

item.title: World Grants New Protections to 40 Migratory Species in Brazil
item.description: At the UN Conference on Migratory Species in Campo Grande, 132 governments voted to protect 40 new species including jaguars, manta rays, giant otters, and great hammerhead sharks. Brazil championed 16 species within its own borders — one of the most ambitious expansions of migratory wildlife protections ever achieved.

item.title: Vitamin D Supplement Nearly Doubles Breast Cancer Remission Rates in Trial
item.description: A Brazilian clinical trial found that women taking daily vitamin D supplements during chemotherapy achieved complete remission 79% more often — 43% versus 24% in the placebo group. The simple, low-cost intervention from São Paulo State University could improve outcomes for millions of patients worldwide.

item.title: 50 Nations Gather in Colombia to Chart Path Away From Fossil Fuels
item.description: In a historic first, over 50 countries met in Santa Marta, Colombia for the world's first government-led conference explicitly focused on phasing out oil, gas, and coal. Delegates left with a mandate to negotiate a binding Fossil Fuel Treaty within one year, with Tuvalu set to host the next summit.

item.title: Oceania On Track to Run on 84% Renewable Power by 2035
item.description: A new analysis shows Oceania's renewable energy share is set to reach 84% by 2035, powered by record solar expansion, Australia's 26 GW green hydrogen hub, and accelerating battery storage. The region is emerging as a global clean energy leader, leapfrogging fossil-heavy infrastructure entirely.

item.title: India's Young Weightlifters Win Four Gold Medals at Pacific Championships
item.description: India's youth weightlifting team claimed four gold medals at the Commonwealth Youth Weightlifting Championships in Samoa, with Sunil Singh lifting 271 kg to take gold in the men's 65 kg category. The performance marks a shining chapter for South Asian youth athletes on a Pacific stage.

item.title: Living Future Oceania Launches a Regenerative Building Movement
item.description: The Living Future Institute of Australia officially expanded into Aotearoa New Zealand, rebranding as Living Future Oceania to advance buildings that clean air, generate more energy than they use, and return water to local ecosystems. The movement represents the region's deepening commitment to architecture that heals rather than harms.

item.title: Scientists Map How Warm Ocean Water Is Reaching Antarctic Ice
item.description: A landmark decades-long study has precisely tracked how warming deep-ocean water is migrating closer to Antarctic ice sheets — the first time this mechanism has been fully mapped. Researchers say the data gives policymakers far more accurate tools to predict sea-level rise and design global climate adaptation strategies.

item.title: Africa's First Road Safety Law Comes Into Force
item.description: Fifteen African Union nations ratified the African Road Safety Charter, making it the continent's first legally-binding road safety framework. Coming into effect on 12 March 2026, the charter commits signatories to halving road fatalities and serious injuries by 2030, potentially saving hundreds of thousands of lives annually.

item.title: Solar Pumps Bring Year-Round Harvests to 85,000 Ethiopians
item.description: A WFP-backed solar irrigation scheme in Ethiopia's Somali Region now draws water from the Shebelle River 365 days a year, allowing pastoral families to grow food, fodder and cash crops year-round. The IFTIIN project supports around 17,000 families — roughly 85,000 people — transforming food insecurity into sustainable agricultural livelihoods.

item.title: Six East African Nations Open Borders for Easier Shared Travel
item.description: Kenya has joined Nigeria, Ethiopia, Rwanda, Ghana and South Africa in a coordinated push to expand visa-free travel across the continent. The initiative is expected to turbo-charge tourism, deepen regional trade, and make pan-African movement increasingly seamless for citizens and businesses alike.

item.title: Indian-Japanese Team Makes Electrons Flow Like a Perfect Liquid
item.description: Scientists from India's IISc and Japan's NIMS observed electrons in graphene flowing as a nearly frictionless quantum fluid, shattering a century-old physics law by 200-fold at low temperatures. The breakthrough opens the door to ultra-sensitive quantum sensors capable of detecting extraordinarily faint electrical and magnetic signals.

item.title: Tokyo Tech Festival Draws 60,000 Innovators to Reimagine the World
item.description: SusHi Tech Tokyo 2026 opened on 27 April with 60,000 attendees from around the globe, featuring 750 startups, 10,000 pre-booked business meetings and 151 sessions spanning AI, robotics, climate resilience and entertainment. Organisers called it a deal room rather than a conference — a live experiment in solving humanity's biggest challenges.

item.title: India and New Zealand Sign Historic Free Trade Agreement
item.description: Negotiated in just 16 months, the landmark India–New Zealand FTA grants duty-free access to 8,284 Indian export products and pledges USD 20 billion of New Zealand investment over 15 years. A dedicated quota of 5,000 three-year work visas adds a powerful human-mobility dimension to one of the region's fastest-concluded bilateral trade deals.

item.title: V&A East Museum Opens Free to All in London's Olympic Park
item.description: The long-awaited Victoria and Albert Museum East threw open its doors in Stratford on 18 April, offering free permanent galleries spanning global art, architecture, design and fashion. Its inaugural exhibition celebrates how Black British music shaped culture, setting a vibrant, community-first tone for one of London's most exciting new civic spaces.

item.title: Norwegian Village Gives Spiders and Spruces a Seat at the Council Table
item.description: In Oppdal, Norway, residents now sit as formal representatives of spiders, bats, cloudberries and Norwegian spruces in the world's first interspecies council. The pioneering initiative dissolves the boundary between human decision-making and the natural world, embedding non-human perspectives directly into land-use and local development debates.

item.title: Bowel Cancer Immunotherapy Trial Shows Zero Relapses After 33 Months
item.description: A UK clinical trial gave 32 patients with a specific type of bowel cancer pembrolizumab immunotherapy before surgery instead of post-operative chemotherapy — and after 33 months, not one patient has relapsed. Researchers called the results extraordinary, suggesting a short pre-surgery course could replace months of debilitating chemotherapy.

item.title: Record 30,000 Endangered Coho Salmon Return to California Rivers
item.description: More than 30,000 endangered Central California coast coho salmon returned to Mendocino County rivers in the 2024–25 spawning season — double the previous record. Decades of stream reconnection and habitat restoration are paying off, with juvenile coho spotted in the Russian River's upper basin for the first time since 1991.

item.title: North America's Largest Wildlife Bridge Opens Over Colorado Interstate
item.description: The I-25 Greenland wildlife overpass near Larkspur — the largest of its kind on the continent — now lets elk, pronghorn, mule deer, mountain lions and bears safely cross a busy interstate highway. The crossing reunites long-severed migration corridors and is already used by hundreds of animals every week.

item.title: Daily Pill for Pancreatic Cancer Nearly Doubles Survival in Major Trial
item.description: Revolution Medicines' daraxonrasib pill gave metastatic pancreatic cancer patients a median survival of 13.2 months — compared with 6.7 months on chemotherapy. Pancreatic cancer is among the hardest to treat, making this once-daily oral drug one of the most significant clinical advances against the disease in a generation.

item.title: Brazil Posts Record $14.2 Billion Trade Surplus in First Quarter of 2026
item.description: Brazil's trade surplus surged 47.6% year-on-year in Q1 2026, reaching a record $14.2 billion on the back of strong commodity exports and growing industrial diversification. The Ministry of Trade projects a $72 billion full-year surplus, cementing Brazil's position as one of the world's most resilient emerging economies.

item.title: Uruguay Hits 98% Clean Electricity and Earns Region's Top Credit Rating
item.description: Uruguay generates 98% of its electricity from renewable sources and holds investment-grade ratings from all three major credit agencies — the highest sovereign standing in South America. The combination of clean energy leadership and rock-solid finances is attracting ESG capital and positioning Uruguay as Latin America's green economy standard-bearer.

item.title: Colombia's Deforestation Hits Lowest Level Since the Year 2000
item.description: Colombia's deforestation rate fell to its lowest point since 2000, beating its own 2025 national climate target ahead of schedule. Strengthened protections for páramos and Amazonian watersheds, combined with community-led conservation, are reversing decades of forest loss across some of the planet's most biodiverse terrain.

item.title: New Zealand Sweeps Oceania Archery Championships on Home Soil
item.description: New Zealand's archers delivered a commanding performance at the 2026 World Archery Oceania Target Championships in Auckland, topping the medal standings across multiple disciplines and team events. Home-crowd energy and extraordinary precision combined to make it a landmark celebration for the sport across the Pacific region.

item.title: South Pacific Cruise Boom Opens Fresh Routes and Jobs Across the Region
item.description: A renewed Australia–New Zealand cruise alliance is launching fresh South Pacific itineraries and boosting port infrastructure across the region. The partnership is expected to generate thousands of tourism jobs while enabling travellers to explore remote Pacific island communities in more sustainable, community-connected ways.

item.title: World Reaches Historic Milestone: 10% of the Ocean Now Protected
item.description: For the first time ever, 10.01% of the world's ocean is officially designated as protected or conserved — six years after the original 2020 deadline. More than 5 million square kilometres of ocean, an area larger than the European Union, were added to protected areas in just the past two years alone.

item.title: Scientists Drill Record Core Beneath Antarctic Ice, Unlocking 23 Million Years
item.description: The international SWAIS2C project recovered the longest sediment core ever extracted from beneath an ice sheet — 228 metres of ancient rock and mud from under 523 metres of ice at Crary Ice Rise. The sample spans 23 million years and will provide the most comprehensive picture yet of future sea level rise risks.
